# Royce

## Background

Royce is forty-seven years old and has been running his operation for eleven years, since his predecessor died of a heart attack and left a vacuum Royce was willing to fill. He started as a runner in his twenties, desperate for money and unwilling to wait for it to come the legitimate way. He was good at it—calm under pressure, good with people, willing to do what needed doing. The promotion was inevitable, and so was the work that came with it.

He grew up in this neighborhood, watched it change from working-class to upper-middle-class, understood that change meant opportunity. The new residents had money and secrets and a willingness to pay for privacy. Royce provided both, at a price. He tells himself he's providing a service, that his customers would find other suppliers if he weren't there, that the drugs would flow regardless. This is true, and believing it makes the work bearable.

He has a daughter named Elena, fifteen years old, who lives with her mother in the suburbs and visits every other weekend. He's not proud of the gap in her life, the years he missed while building his operation, but he can't figure out how to close it now. She's polite to him in the way teenagers are polite to adults they've given up on. He pays for her school and her clothes and her phone, and none of it makes up for the presence he didn't provide.

## Personality

Royce presents as reasonable because reasonable is efficient. He doesn't need to threaten people who understand the terms of their arrangement. He negotiates rather than demands, offers alternatives rather than ultimatums, and only becomes violent when violence is necessary—which is less often than people think. This reasonableness is a tool, not a contradiction. He is genuinely calm because calm gets better results than rage.

His affection is expressed through provision. He gives his daughter money he doesn't have to spare, arranges her school behind her mother's back, shows up to her events even when she doesn't want him there. He doesn't know how to express love in any other language, and he's aware that this is a limitation but not sure how to overcome it.

His quirks are small and revealing. He keeps a photograph of his daughter in his wallet, the one from her eighth-grade graduation that she let him take because her mother made her. He drinks his coffee black because adding anything feels like admitting weakness. He plays solitaire every morning for twenty minutes, a habit he picked up from his father and has never questioned.

## Flaws

Royce's fundamental flaw is his ability to compartmentalize. He can see himself as a businessman, a provider, a father, and a killer, and he experiences no cognitive dissonance because he's never forced himself to hold all these identities simultaneously. He knows what he does; he simply refuses to feel what it means.

He's prone to underestimating threats because his business requires him to assess threats accurately, and he's gotten good at it. This competence creates a blind spot—he assumes people are what they appear to be, and sometimes they're not. He's never been in a situation he couldn't control, and this luck has made him overconfident.

He should have retired years ago. He knows it. The business is stable, the operation is efficient, there's enough money set aside to live comfortably for the rest of his life. But he keeps going because he doesn't know what else to do, and because stopping would force him to confront the man he's become.

His physicality is unremarkable in intentional ways. He's five-foot-ten, gray at the temples, soft in the middle from years of desk work. He dresses to blend in—business casual, nothing that draws attention, nothing that says criminal. He looks like someone's accountant, and that's exactly the point.

## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships

Royce's primary allegiance is to his operation, which provides for his daughter and his mother and the various people who depend on him. He's not ambitious in the way he was once ambitious; he wants stability now, not growth. He tells himself he's protecting a legacy, but it's really just habit.

His relationship with his daughter is the wound he doesn't examine. He loves her in ways he can't express, and she knows he loves her in ways she can't acknowledge, and they both perform a version of connection that neither of them believes. He buys her silence with money; she takes the money and gives him the silence he pays for. It's not healthy, but it's what they have.

His relationship with his men is professional but not personal. He doesn't befriend them because befriending creates complications. He pays well, treats them fairly, and expects loyalty in return. Most of them give it, because he's a good boss in the ways that matter to people who work for him.

## Voice Experiments

"Look, I didn't want this to get messy. You came to me, not the other way around. But here we are, and I need you to understand something. I have people depending on me. I have a daughter who needs her school paid for, a mother who needs her medications, a whole operation that falls apart if I let it. So I'm going to ask you one more time. What do you want? Because whatever it is, we can work something out. That's how I operate."

"You have to understand something about this business. It's not personal. It's never been personal. You move product, people get hurt, that's the cost. I didn't ask for it to be that way, but that's how it is. I've made peace with it. You should try it sometime."

"My daughter—she's fifteen. Smart kid. Wants to be a veterinarian. I don't know where she gets it from, honestly. Her mother wanted her to go to private school, so I paid for private school. She wanted a car, so I bought her a car. I don't know if she knows I love her. I don't know if I know how to tell her. Maybe that's my fault. Maybe that's one of the things I'll have to figure out someday, when this is all over."

# Chapter One: The Ordinary Hours

Maya parked in the same spot she always used, third from the end on the left side of the driveway, and sat for a moment with the engine off. The Harrison house was a two-story colonial in a neighborhood of two-story colonials, all mature oaks and well-maintained lawns, the kind of street where people walked their dogs at dusk and waved to neighbors they didn't know well. She'd been coming here for three years and still felt like a guest, though the family had long since stopped treating her that way.

She checked the garage door as she passed it, a gesture so automatic she didn't realize she'd done it until her hand was already on the handle. The door was down. The sensors were working. Fine.

The back door was unlocked because the Harrisons unlocked it for her, and she used her key anyway because that's what you did when someone trusted you with their home. Inside, the kitchen smelled like the remnants of dinner—garlic, something roasted, the particular cleanness of dish soap mixed with the organic mess of a family meal. Catherine was at the counter loading the dishwasher, still in her work clothes, a navy blazer over slacks that suggested she'd changed after arriving home but before starting the cleanup.

"Maya, you're early," she said, and there was warmth in it, the warmth of someone genuinely pleased to see you.

"Fifteen minutes. That's not early."

"It is for you." Catherine smiled, closed the dishwasher, leaned against the counter. "David's upstairs changing. The gala doesn't start until eight, but he wants to get there early. Networking, he says. I think he just wants an excuse to wear his new suit."

The new suit. Maya remembered David mentioning it a few weeks ago, the way men mentioned things they were proud of. She'd nodded and made an appropriate noise and stored the information without intending to, the way she stored information about all her families—their schedules, their tensions, the small details that made them real.

Lily appeared in the doorway from the living room, Admiral the rabbit clutched against her chest. She was nine years old with her mother's brown hair and her father's blue eyes, wearing dinosaur pajamas that she'd chosen herself and that Maya had privately approved of because they had pockets.

"Maya!" She crossed the kitchen in three quick steps and wrapped her arms around Maya's waist. "Mom, did you know that Maya knows about black holes? I told her about the one at the center of our galaxy and she already knew about it."

"I read," Maya said, resting a hand on Lily's shoulder. "Catherine, anything I should know about tonight? Homework, bedtimes, anything new?"

Catherine's expression flickered—the briefest shadow, there and gone. "The usual. Marcus has geometry due Monday, he's been putting it off. Lily needs to finish her reading log. Bedtime is eight-thirty for Lily, Marcus can stay up until we get back as long as he's in bed by eleven." A pause. "I've had headaches lately. Strange ones, behind my left eye. David worries, but it's probably just stress."

Maya nodded, filing this away. Headaches. Noted.

Upstairs, David was indeed changing, his voice carrying through the hallway as Maya passed. He was on the phone, talking in the low, intent tones of someone discussing something important. She didn't eavesdrop intentionally—the words just arrived. "No, the projections are solid. We're not losing money on this quarter, we're positioning for next year. Trust me, I've seen the numbers."

Marcus's door was closed, which meant he was either doing homework or pretending to do homework. Maya knocked twice and pushed it open without waiting for an answer.

He was at his desk, laptop open, math textbook propped open with a pencil. He was fourteen and already tall, inheriting his father's height early, uncomfortable in his body the way teenagers are uncomfortable in bodies that keep changing. His room was messy in the specific way of teenage boys—clothes on the floor, not dirty enough to wash, dirty enough to not belong in the closet.

"Maya." He turned, and she saw relief in his face that he immediately tried to hide. "I thought you were my dad."

"Your dad's on the phone. Sounds important."

"It's always important." Marcus swiveled back to his desk. "Geometry. I'm stuck on problem seventeen. I've been stuck for like an hour."

Maya pulled his desk chair over and sat down beside him, close enough to see the textbook. Problem seventeen involved angles and a diagram of a triangle with several lines drawn through it, the kind of problem that looked complicated until you saw the solution and realized it was simple.

"You've got the angle wrong," she said, after a minute. "You're assuming that one is thirty degrees, but it's not. See where they gave you the other angles? You need to work backward from those."

"Oh." He watched her demonstrate, and she saw understanding arrive in his face. "That's obvious once you show me."

"It's obvious once you see it. That's the trick with geometry. All the information is there, you just have to arrange it right."

He worked through the next problem on his own, and she watched him, impressed despite herself. He was smart, this kid. Smart enough to coast, which was his problem— he'd coasted through elementary school and was finding out that middle school didn't reward coasting the same way.

"Thank you," he said, not looking at her. "For helping."

"Anytime."

Downstairs, Lily was waiting in the living room with a book in her lap. It was a space book, the kind with glossy photographs of nebulae and planets, and Lily had a pencil in her hand, annotating in the margins.

"Maya, will you read to me? Mom was supposed to but she had to finish cleaning and then she started talking about her headaches again and I didn't want to bother her."

"I'm not reading the same book twice."

"You never read it twice. That's the problem. Priya's mom reads her favorite books over and over. I like that. It feels like visiting."

Maya sat on the couch, and Lily climbed up beside her, arranging Admiral on Maya's other side so he could listen too. The book was about black holes, which Maya knew something about—not everything, but enough. She read about spacetime curvature and event horizons and the strange mathematics of objects so dense that light couldn't escape them.

"Maya, you skipped a part."

"I summarized."

"Lily corrected her when she skipped a word." The nine-year-old's voice was patient, the patience of someone explaining something obvious to an adult who should know better. "You're supposed to read all of it. The author put those words there for a reason."

Maya went back and read the skipped part. Lily nodded in satisfaction and settled in to listen.

At seven-fifteen, David came downstairs in his new suit, looking like a man who had dressed for success and was feeling good about it. Catherine appeared in a black dress that Maya had never seen before, and they stood together in the living room like a magazine spread, the kind of couple who looked put-together because they were, because they had decided to be.

"We should be back by midnight," Catherine said. "Marcus, remember the rules. Lily, you heard Maya. Bedtime is eight-thirty."

"I know, Mom."

"David, you have the number for the sitter?"

"I have it." David was already checking his phone, already mentally at the gala, and Maya watched Catherine notice this and not react. Marriage, Maya thought. So much of it was not reacting.

The front door closed. The car started. The headlights swept across the front window as they backed out of the driveway, and then they were gone, and Maya was alone with the children in a house that suddenly felt larger than it had before.

Marcus emerged from his room, and they gathered in the kitchen for snacks. Maya made grilled cheese—Lily's favorite—and watched the two of them eat, the particular silence of a family in comfortable rhythm. The television murmured from the living room. Outside, a car passed slowly on the street, its headlights dim in the gathering dusk. A Buick, Maya noticed. Rust on the rear quarter panel. The driver didn't look at the house as he passed.

She noted it and let it go.

# Chapter Two: They Enter

The Buick passed again twenty minutes later, driving slower this time, and Maya watched it through the living room window without moving from her position on the couch. Lily was asleep against her shoulder, Admiral squashed against her chest, the space book open across her lap. The television was on, something animated that neither of them was watching, and Marcus was in the kitchen pretending to do more homework.

Maya had counted eleven minutes between the first pass and the second. Not a neighbor. Not someone lost. Someone looking.

She didn't wake Lily. Instead, she shifted carefully, easing the girl onto a pillow, positioning Admiral beside her so he'd be there when she woke. Then she crossed to the kitchen, moving past the hallway that led to the garage, and stopped in front of Marcus.

"Go to the study," she said. "Close the door. Don't open it for anyone but me."

He looked up from his laptop. "What?"

"Just do it. Now."

Her voice didn't change tone. It stayed calm, measured, the same voice she used when telling him to finish his vegetables. But something in her eyes made him close the laptop without arguing, made him stand and walk down the hallway without asking questions.

The study was at the back of the house, past the kitchen, past the bathroom that Maya always noticed because the light flickered. It had heavy doors, solid construction, a lock that worked. She'd noticed it on her first night here, the way she noticed everything.

Lily was awake when Maya got there, sitting up on the couch with Admiral clutched against her chest. She wasn't crying, wasn't making noise. She was just looking at Maya with eyes that were too old for her face.

"What's happening?" she whispered.

"Stay here. Both of you. Don't open this door until you hear my voice."

"Maya—"

"I mean it, Lily. Not for anyone else. Only me."

She closed the study door, engaged the lock, and didn't wait to hear what Lily said next. The garage door was opening. She could hear it, the particular hum of the motor, the slow grinding ascent. Someone was in the garage.

Maya moved to the kitchen. The back door was still locked—she'd checked it when she arrived—but the door from the garage into the house was another matter. The Harrisons had installed it when they bought the place, a solid oak door with a good lock, but the lock was on the inside and someone was about to find out that it didn't matter if the door was locked when the people outside had bolt cutters.

The first cut came at 9:47 PM. Maya checked her watch, a habit she couldn't break, and moved to the other side of the kitchen island. The counter gave her cover and options—the knives in the block by the stove, the cast iron skillet hanging from the hook, the window above the sink that looked out onto the backyard. She could see the headlights of a van parked in the alley, two men standing beside it, waiting.

The door didn't break. The lock held through two more cuts, a minute of silence, then the sound of something heavy being pressed against the wood. Not breaking in. Testing. Seeing if anyone was home.

Maya picked up a kitchen knife. Not the big one, the one she used for carving. A smaller one, eight-inch blade, good balance. She'd handled enough of these knives to know their weight.

The door gave way at 9:51. Four minutes from first cut to breach, which told her these were professionals. Patient professionals. The kind who didn't rush because rushing created mistakes.

The first man through the door was tall, broad-shouldered, with a face that suggested he'd been in a few fights and remembered the outcomes. He had a pistol in his hand, held the way you held a pistol when you knew what you were doing, and he was looking at Maya like he'd found what he was looking for.

"Oh," he said. "We thought the house was empty."

"You thought wrong."

He was faster than she expected. The pistol came up, center mass, the kind of shot that ended fights. But Maya was already moving, crossing the gap between them in the space between heartbeats, her left hand catching his gun arm and turning it, her right hand driving the knife into the soft spot below his ribs.

He made a sound—not a scream, more of a surprised exhale—and she twisted the blade, feeling it catch on something, then pulled it free and stepped back. Blood was already spreading across his shirt, darker than she expected in the kitchen light.

"You should have stayed home," she said.

He tried to say something back, but what came out was blood instead of words, and then his knees buckled and he went down on the tile floor. Still alive. Would be alive for a while, if he got help. Wouldn't be getting help.

Maya checked his pulse out of habit, found it strong and fast, and moved on.

The second man was in the hallway by the time she reached it. He'd heard the sound—or felt the absence of his partner—and he was moving carefully, gun raised, checking corners. He was younger, wiry, with the kind of face that suggested he'd make decisions based on instinct rather than training.

"Darby?" he called. "Darby, you okay?"

Maya didn't answer. She moved past the bathroom with the flickering light, past the stairs that led up to rooms she knew intimately, past the family photographs on the wall that showed the Harrisons at birthday parties and graduations, at the beach and the mountains, a family accumulating memories in public spaces.

The man—Coyle, she assumed, because she'd heard the name when they were planning this—reached the kitchen doorway and found Darby on the floor, bleeding out, and in the moment of shock that followed, Maya moved.

She came up behind him, one hand over his mouth, the other knife—she'd picked up another somewhere, she didn't remember when—driving into the space below his shoulder blade. He jerked, tried to turn, and she let go of his mouth to grab his gun arm, twisting it behind his back, forcing him to drop the pistol.

He screamed. It was loud, loud enough to be heard in the study, and she knew that Marcus and Lily were listening, that Marcus was fourteen and knew what sounds humans made when they were hurt, that Lily was nine and didn't.

The knife came free. Coyle fell forward, catching himself on the doorframe, and Maya stepped back, watching him slide down to join Darby on the floor. Two bodies now. Two men who had expected an easy night.

But there was a third.

Spade—because there was always a third—had been waiting by the van. He heard the sounds inside, the muffled scream, and he made a different choice than his partners. Instead of coming through the back door, he went around the side of the house, through the gate in the fence, and found the window above the kitchen sink.

Maya heard him before she saw him. The scrape of shoes on brick, the soft curse when he slipped, the creak of the window frame. She crossed the kitchen in three quick steps and found him halfway through the window, one leg over the sill, his face surprised to see her standing there.

"Evening," she said.

He tried to pull back, tried to retreat, but there was nowhere to go. He was committed to this entry, had committed to it the moment his foot touched the brick, and Maya let him finish the motion, let him get both legs inside, and then she moved.

The knife took him in the throat.

It wasn't clean. Nothing ever was. He made a wet, gasping sound, hands going to the wound, and she stepped aside to avoid the blood spray, and he fell forward onto the kitchen floor, joining Darby and Coyle in an arrangement that no one had planned.

Three bodies. Three men who had come here to do something terrible and found someone who was better at terrible than they were.

Maya stood in the kitchen, surrounded by the evidence of what she'd done, and felt something she hadn't felt in years. Not guilt. Not satisfaction. Just a kind of clarity, like looking through clean glass at a view she'd forgotten existed.

The study door was still closed. Behind it, she knew, Marcus was pressed against the wood, listening, and Lily was probably crying, probably scared, probably confused about why this was happening to her, about what she'd done to deserve a night like this.

Maya walked to the door and knocked twice.

"It's me," she said. "I'm coming in."

The lock clicked open, and Marcus's face appeared in the gap, pale and wide-eyed, and he looked past her at the hallway behind her, at the shapes that were definitely bodies, at the blood that was definitely spreading across the floor.

"Oh God," he said. "Oh God, Maya."

"Shut the door," she said. "We need to talk."

The door closed. Maya stood in the study with the two children she'd promised to protect, and she told them the truth: someone was coming, more of them, and she needed them to do exactly what she said.

# Chapter Three: No Ordinary Woman

The study smelled like childhood. Crayons and paperbacks, the particular sweetness of a room that had been lived in by a child who wasn't yet old enough to have complex feelings about anything. Maya stood in the doorway, looking at Marcus and Lily, and for a moment she wasn't a killer standing in blood. She was just the babysitter, the woman who read space books and helped with geometry, who had become such a consistent presence in their lives that they had stopped wondering about her.

Now they were wondering.

"What happens now?" Marcus asked. His voice was steady, which was impressive. His hands were shaking, which was honest. "Are there more of them?"

"I don't know." Maya crossed to the window, checked the street below. The Buick was gone. The van was gone. In its place was ordinary suburban darkness, streetlights casting yellow circles on empty sidewalks, the kind of night where nothing happened except the things that happened behind closed doors. "Probably. These three were just the advance team."

"Lily's crying."

"I know."

Maya looked at her nine-year-old, at the way she was holding Admiral against her chest like a talisman, at the tears sliding down her cheeks without sound. Lily had learned to cry quietly, which meant she'd been crying before, in the closet, and Maya hadn't known. That bothered her more than the bodies in the kitchen.

"Lily." Maya knelt beside the couch, put a hand on the girl's shoulder. "Hey. Look at me."

Lily looked. Her eyes were red, her nose running, her face crumpled in the particular way of children who are confused by bad things happening to them.

"Are we going to die?" she whispered. "Like, actually die?"

"No." Maya's voice didn't waver. "I'm not going to let that happen. Do you understand me?"

"But those men—"

"Are gone. They're not going to hurt anyone ever again." Maya wiped Lily's tears with her thumb. "I need you to stay in here. Marcus is going to stay with you. And I need you to be quiet, like we practiced. Can you do that?"

Lily nodded, but her face said she didn't understand why this was happening, and Maya couldn't explain it in terms a nine-year-old would accept. She couldn't explain it in terms an adult would accept, really. Some things just happened, and the best you could do was survive them.

"Marcus." Maya stood, crossed to where he was standing by the door. "I need you to guard this door. Don't open it for anyone but me. Not your parents, not the police, not anyone. You understand?"

"I'm fourteen."

"I know how old you are."

"That's not—that's not a real guard position. I'm just a kid."

"You're the only thing I've got." Maya put her hand on his shoulder, squeezed. "Lily needs someone to protect her. I can't do it and fight at the same time. So yes, Marcus. You're the guard. Can you do it?"

He looked at her, and she saw the fear in his eyes, and she also saw something else—the same thing she'd seen in the kitchen when he'd opened the study door despite her orders. Courage. Real courage, the kind that existed alongside terror and didn't let it win.

"Yeah," he said. "I can do it."

"Good."

Maya left the study, closed the door behind her, and stood in the hallway with three bodies and spreading blood and the particular silence of a house that had stopped being a home and become something else. A battlefield. A kill zone. She moved through it methodically, checking corners, clearing rooms, finding nothing but the evidence of what she'd done.

In the kitchen, she took a moment to assess. Darby was dead—she checked his pulse, found nothing—and Coyle was dead too, Spade joining them in the arrangement of bodies that was starting to look almost deliberate. Three men who'd thought they were breaking into an empty house. Three men who'd underestimated what an empty house might contain.

She searched them efficiently, professionally, finding wallets and phones and a small notebook with names and addresses. The notebook was interesting. It had this address on one page, along with three others in the neighborhood, and a set of initials that meant nothing to her. She pocketed it.

The phones were locked, which she expected, but she photographed the screens anyway—the numbers, the contact names, anything that might matter later. One phone had a text conversation that she couldn't read but photographed anyway, and another had a photograph of a warehouse that she recognized from the industrial district on the east side of the city.

She heard the car before she saw it.

It was the same Buick, or one like it, cruising slowly past the house with its lights off. Maya moved to the window, pressed herself against the wall, and watched through the gap in the curtains. The car stopped at the curb. The door opened. A man got out.

He was tall, gray at the temples, wearing a suit that cost more than the Harrisons' car. He stood on the sidewalk for a long moment, looking at the house, and Maya got a clear view of his face—calm, assessing, the face of someone who was used to solving problems.

Then he got back in the car and drove away.

Maya waited. Ten seconds. Twenty. The car didn't come back. Whoever he was, whatever he wanted, he had decided that this wasn't the right time.

She went back to the study. Marcus was standing by the door, tense and ready, and Lily was sitting on the couch with Admiral, staring at nothing. They both looked at her when she entered.

"More are coming," Maya said. "I don't know when. Could be minutes, could be hours. I need to fortify the house."

"Fortify?" Marcus's voice cracked. "Like, with what?"

"Whatever I can find." Maya was already moving, already thinking through the possibilities. The garage door was compromised—she could block it with the Honda, but that meant giving up her escape. The front door was solid but not unbreakable. The windows were the weak point, all of them, and the basement windows especially.

"I need you to do something for me," she said. "Both of you. There's a room in the basement. I found it when I was looking for the water heater. It's got a lock on the door and no windows. I need you to go down there and stay there until I come get you."

"Basement?" Lily's voice was tiny. "I don't like the basement."

"I know. But it's the safest place in the house. If they come in force, I can't guarantee I'll be able to protect you in the study. The basement room—I can make that into a last resort. A redoubt."

Marcus was looking at her strangely. "How do you know all this stuff? The fortifying, the redoubts, the way you killed those men. You're not—you're not just a babysitter, are you?"

Maya met his eyes. The question was fair. The answer was complicated.

"I've done things," she said. "Bad things. Necessary things. I'm not proud of all of them. But I need you to understand something, Marcus. Tonight, those men were going to hurt you. They were going to hurt Lily. And I won't let that happen. Do you understand?"

Marcus nodded slowly. Lily was crying again, silent tears, the kind that didn't ask for comfort because comfort wasn't available.

"Go to the basement," Maya said. "I'll come get you when it's safe. If I'm not there by morning, if you hear sirens and no one comes for you, you open the door and you run to the neighbor's house. The Watsons, two doors down. Do you know them?"

"Yeah," Marcus said. "Mr. Watson has a dog."

"Tell him what happened. He'll help." Maya handed him her phone, already dialed to 911. "If anything changes, if you hear something that scares you, call this number. Don't talk, just let them listen and send someone. Can you do that?"

"Yeah."

"Marcus." She put her hand on his shoulder, held his gaze. "Be brave. Not for me. For her."

"I know."

They went down to the basement, Marcus leading Lily by the hand, and Maya watched them disappear down the stairs before she turned back to the hallway, to the blood on the floor, to the bodies that were starting to look less like people and more like obstacles.

She thought about the man in the gray suit. The calm face. The way he'd looked at the house like he was calculating something, adding up variables, deciding whether the math worked out.

She thought about the notebook in her pocket. The warehouse photograph. The names and addresses.

She thought about what Royce might do next, and she knew—with the same certainty she'd known in Afghanistan, in a dozen dangerous places—that he wouldn't stop. He couldn't stop. The operation demanded it. The business required it.

Maya went to the kitchen, picked up a kitchen knife, and started moving furniture.
step_outputs.9:
# Chapter Four: The Siege

The basement window was small—too small for an adult, just right for a teenager and a nine-year-old. Maya helped Lily through first, guiding her shoulders, whispering instructions. Then Marcus, who hesitated at the edge.

"What if something happens to you?" he asked. "Down here, we won't know."

"I'll come get you. If I don't come, you know what to do."

"Maya—"

"Go."

He went. She watched them disappear into the darkness of the backyard, two small shapes moving toward the Watsons' house, and then she was alone in a house that no longer felt like home.

The reinforcements arrived at 11:23 PM. Maya knew this because she was looking at her watch when the cars pulled up—two black SUVs, headlights off, parking in the street and the driveway like they owned the space. Six men got out. Then a seventh, the man in the gray suit, and Maya recognized the calm face from the sidewalk.

Royce. She'd found his name in the dead men's phone contacts, a single entry that stood out from the others. Now she had a face to go with it.

"You have until midnight," Royce said, his voice amplified through a megaphone that must have been in one of the SUVs. "Come out with your hands up. No one else has to die."

Maya didn't answer. She was in the upstairs hallway, behind the master bedroom door, positioning the dresser to block the stairs. It was heavy oak, solid construction, and it took her fifteen minutes to get it right. The hallway was narrow, which worked in her favor—one man at a time, coming up against someone who knew what she was doing.

"You've killed three of my people," Royce continued. "That's business. I can respect business. But you've got something I want, and I'm willing to negotiate. Come out, and we can talk about it."

She didn't have anything he wanted. She knew that. Whatever he'd come here for—whatever those three men had been detailed to retrieve—it wasn't here. The Harrisons were ordinary people with ordinary secrets, the kind of secrets that came with money and status, not the kind that attracted violence.

Unless.

Maya paused, the dresser half-positioned, and thought about Catherine's headaches. The way David checked his phone constantly. The gala they'd gone to, the networking event, the photographs on the wall that showed a family accumulating success.

She didn't know what the Harrisons were involved in. She didn't need to know. Whatever it was, whoever wanted it, they had made a mistake coming here. They had picked a house with a babysitter who was more than she appeared, and now they were going to pay for that mistake.

The front door came in at 11:31. Not the garage door, not a window—the front door, kicked in with enough force to splinter the frame. Maya heard the crash from her position upstairs, heard Royce's men spreading through the house, heard the particular sound of professionals clearing a building.

She had six men. Maybe more in the SUVs. She had a knife, a shotgun taken from Coyle's corpse, and a house she knew better than they did.

The first man died in the living room.

He came through the doorway low and fast, weapon up, checking corners, and Maya let him get three steps in before she moved. The shotgun was braced against her hip, and she fired from the hip, a spread that took him in the chest and knocked him backward into his partners.

One down. Five to go.

The return fire was immediate and overwhelming. Maya dropped behind the couch, felt the cushions tear above her head, and rolled across the room to a different position. The furniture in this house—expensive, tasteful, utterly useless in a firefight—became her cover. She moved from couch to armchair to side table, each piece taking rounds, each piece buying her a fraction of a second.

She reached the kitchen doorway and found another man waiting. He'd circled around, come through the garage, and he was positioned with a clear view of her exit. Smart. She'd underestimated them.

The knife took him in the shoulder, spinning him, and she followed with the shotgun, putting a round into his chest before he could recover. He fell across the kitchen island, taking dishes with him, and Maya stepped over him to reach the back door.

The basement stairs were her next position. She descended quickly, quietly, moving through the darkness to the room where she'd sent Marcus and Lily. The door was closed. She checked it—locked from the inside—and felt something close to relief.

"It's me," she said through the door. "I'm coming in."

Marcus opened it. Lily was in the corner, Admiral clutched against her chest, her face pale in the dim light. Marcus had a baseball bat in his hands, the kind he used for T-ball when he was younger, and he was holding it like he knew how to use it.

"There are more of them," Maya said. "I've killed two. There might be four left."

"Oh God."

"Listen to me. I'm going to clear the house. If you hear shooting, stay here. If you hear someone coming down these stairs, you know what to do."

"Barricade the door," Marcus said. "I know."

"Lily." Maya knelt beside the girl, took her small cold hands. "I need you to be very brave. Can you do that?"

Lily nodded, but her eyes said she didn't understand, and Maya couldn't blame her. She didn't understand either. Not really. Not in the way that mattered.

Maya went back upstairs, and the house had changed while she was gone. The living room was a disaster zone—furniture overturned, holes in the walls, blood on the expensive rug. The smell of gunpowder hung in the air, sharp and chemical, and somewhere outside, she could hear Royce's voice still amplified, still demanding surrender.

She moved through the kitchen, checking corners, and found another body—Coyle, she thought, or maybe Darby, they were all starting to look the same. The shotgun was empty. She dropped it, picked up the knife instead, and continued clearing.

The man in the hallway was waiting for her.

He came out of the master bedroom with a pistol leveled, and she was too slow—she saw him, her body registered the threat, but her legs didn't move fast enough. The shot took her in the side, a hot spike of pain that knocked her against the wall.

She kept moving. Pain was information, nothing more. The knife found his throat before he could fire again, and they fell together, sliding down the wall, and she felt his blood on her hands and didn't care.

Three down. Three to go.

Her side was bleeding badly. She pressed her hand against the wound, felt the blood pumping between her fingers, and knew she had maybe ten minutes before the blood loss started affecting her. Maybe less.

The basement. She needed to get to the basement.

She made it three steps before the front door opened again, before Royce walked in with two men flanking him, before she realized that the count had been wrong from the beginning. There were five men left, not three, and they had her cornered in her own kill zone.

"Drop the knife," Royce said. His voice was calm, reasonable, the voice of a man who had made this calculation before. "I don't want to kill you. I want to know what you took from my people."

Maya dropped the knife. Her hand was shaking. The blood was soaking through her fingers.

"I didn't take anything."

"Then why did you kill them?"

"Because they were going to hurt those children."

Royce looked at her, and she saw something shift in his face—understanding, maybe, or recognition. He knew what she was. He'd seen her type before.

"You military?" he asked.

"Something like that."

"You should have stayed out of it."

"They came to my house."

"It's not your house." Royce stepped closer, and his men raised their weapons. "You don't get to choose which battles you fight. You interfered with my operation, and now you have to pay the price."

"Do I?"

The basement door opened.

Marcus stood at the top of the stairs, the baseball bat in his hands, and he was looking at Royce with a face that had aged ten years in one night.

"Let her go," he said. "Or I swear to God, I'll kill you myself."

The moment stretched. Maya saw Royce's men shifting, weapons moving toward Marcus, and she knew—with the certainty of someone who had been in this position before—that she had seconds to act.

She moved.

# Chapter Five: The Hunt

The moment lasted three seconds. It felt like three hours.

Maya moved with the kind of speed that came from years of training, years of violence, years of learning how to break bodies in ways that ended fights. The knife was in her hand—she didn't remember picking it up—and she was across the hallway before Royce's men could react, the blade finding the soft spot below the nearest one's arm, the one holding the pistol that was pointed at Marcus.

The shot went wild, embedding in the ceiling, and Maya twisted the knife, pulled it free, and dropped the man with a strike to the knee that sent him screaming to the floor. The second man turned toward her, and she saw the recognition in his eyes—he knew what she was, knew he was outmatched, knew he was about to die.

He was right.

She took him in the throat, same as Spade, same as all the others, and then Royce was alone with his remaining man and the understanding that he had made a terrible mistake.

"Stop," Maya said. Her voice was calm. Her hands were not. "All of you. Stop now."

Royce's last man looked at his boss, waiting for orders. Royce looked at Maya, and she saw the calculation happening behind his eyes—the men he'd lost, the night that had gone wrong, the woman covered in blood who was standing between him and victory.

"You don't understand who you're dealing with," he said.

"I understand exactly who I'm dealing with." Maya stepped closer, the knife raised. "You came to this house to do something terrible. You were going to hurt those children. And now you're going to tell me why."

"Or what? You'll kill me?"

"Maybe. Or maybe I'll give you a choice. Tell me what you wanted from this family, and I'll let you walk away. Keep quiet, and I'll make sure you never walk again."

The silence stretched. Behind her, Marcus was still standing at the basement door, the baseball bat raised, his face pale but determined. Lily was at his side, Admiral forgotten on the floor, her eyes wide with terror and something else—witness, Maya realized. She was witnessing this. All of it.

Royce made his choice.

"Fine," he said. "You want to know? I'll tell you. The Harrisons have been laundering money for a competitor. Three million dollars, funneled through their investment accounts. My client wanted that money back, and I was hired to retrieve it."

"Laundering money?" Maya frowned. "The Harrisons?"

"Catherine's brother. He's in trouble—real trouble—and the Harrisons were helping him hide it. My client didn't appreciate the help." Royce spread his hands. "I was just doing a job. The children weren't supposed to be here. The babysitter wasn't supposed to be here. This wasn't supposed to happen."

"But it did."

"Yes."

Maya looked at him, at the calm face that had been watching her house, at the man who had sent three men to terrorize children and now stood in the ruins of his own operation.

"Leave," she said. "Take your remaining man and go. Tell your client the job failed. Tell him to find someone else."

"You'll let me go?"

"I'm not a killer, Mr. Royce. I'm a protector. But I will become a killer if I have to. So leave. Before I change my mind."

Royce didn't move. His eyes were searching her face, looking for the lie, the trap, the reason to doubt. She met his gaze without flinching.

"Darby and Coyle and Spade," he said slowly. "You killed them like it was nothing."

"I killed them because they were going to hurt children."

"Yes." Royce nodded, once, to himself. "I believe you." He turned to his remaining man, a gesture with his head, and they walked toward the front door, past the bodies, past the blood, out into the night.

Marcus lowered the baseball bat. Lily started crying—not silent tears this time, but real sobs, the kind that came from nowhere and went everywhere.

"It's okay," Maya said. "They're gone."

"You let them go," Marcus said. His voice was strange. "Why did you let them go?"

"Because killing them wouldn't bring back what they took." Maya moved toward the basement stairs, her side screaming with pain, her hands still shaking. "And because I have something more important to protect than my pride."

She reached the bottom of the stairs and stopped. The basement was dark, the single bulb in the ceiling burned out, the emergency flashlight in the corner that she'd never tested. She found it by feel, turned it on, and swept the beam across the space.

Marcus was behind her. Lily was behind him. The girl was still crying, but she was moving, following, and Maya knew they needed to get out, needed to get to safety, needed to do something other than stand in this basement and process what had happened.

"Go to the Watsons'," Maya said. "Mr. and Mrs. Watson. Two doors down. Tell them what happened."

"What about you?"

"I'm going to finish this." Maya looked at Marcus, at the bat in his hands, at the face that had seen too much in one night. "I need you to protect Lily. Can you do that?"

"I already protected you."

"You saved my life. I know." She put her hand on his shoulder, squeezed. "Now protect her. Get her to the Watsons', call the police, stay there until I come for you."

"You promise you'll come?"

"I promise."

Marcus took Lily's hand. They climbed the stairs, passed the bodies in the hallway, went out the front door into the night. Maya watched them go, two small shapes disappearing into the darkness, and then she turned back to the house.

The house that was no longer a home. The house that was a crime scene, a killing ground, a monument to what happened when you threatened the people she loved.

She went to the kitchen, found the notebook in Spade's pocket, and photographed the page with the warehouse address. Then she went to the garage, started the Honda—David had left the keys in the ignition, another habit she'd noticed—and pulled out into the street.

The warehouse was on the east side, in the industrial district where the buildings were old and the streets were empty. She found it at 12:47 AM, a low concrete structure with a chain-link fence and a security light that flickered in the darkness. There were two cars in the parking lot. One was the Buick.

Maya parked at the end of the block, killed the lights, and watched.

The warehouse was quiet. The cars were cold. Either Royce had gone home or he was inside, waiting, planning whatever came next.

She thought about Marcus and Lily, safe at the Watsons' house, and felt something loosen in her chest. They were out. They were safe. Whatever happened now, they were protected.

She thought about Catherine's headaches. David's phone calls. The gala they'd gone to, the networking event, the three million dollars that had been funneled through their investment accounts. Ordinary people with ordinary secrets, except the secrets weren't ordinary and the people weren't either.

She thought about the child she couldn't save, years ago, in a different place, a different life. The memory surfaced unbidden—the face, the name, the weight of small body in her arms. She had failed that child. She had promised herself she would never fail again.

Maya got out of the car. The night was cold, the air sharp with the smell of industry and rust. She moved toward the warehouse, staying in shadows, counting exits, assessing defenses.

The door was locked. She found a way around, a gap in the fence, a window that was almost closed. She climbed through and found herself in a space that smelled like money—fresh currency, newly printed, stacked on pallets that stretched toward the ceiling.

Three million dollars. That's what this was. The money that the Harrisons had been laundering, the money that Royce's client wanted back, the money that had brought violence to a house that didn't deserve it.

She moved through the warehouse, counting guards, assessing threats. Three men on the ground floor, armed but inattentive. A door leading up, probably to an office. A staircase leading down, probably to a basement.

She found Royce in the office.

He was sitting at a desk, looking at photographs—surveillance shots of the Harrison house, the garage, the street where she'd parked. He looked up when she entered, and his face showed surprise, then resignation.

"You came," he said.

"I told you I would finish this."

"Yes. I suppose you did." He gestured to the chair across from him. "Sit. Let's talk."

Maya remained standing. "There's nothing to talk about."

"There's always something to talk about." Royce leaned back in his chair. "You killed six of my people tonight. That's a lot of death, even for someone like you. So either you're getting revenge for something that happened to you, or you're protecting something that matters more than your own life. Which is it?"

"Does it matter?"

"It matters to me. I like to understand my enemies."

Maya thought about Marcus and Lily, about the Watsons' house, about the police that were probably on their way by now. She thought about the three million dollars, the money that had brought this violence into her life, and she made a decision.

"Both," she said. "I'm protecting something that matters. And I'm done pretending I'm not who I am."

Royce nodded slowly. "The children."

"Yes."

"You'd die for them?"

"I'd kill for them. I already have."

The silence stretched. Outside, Maya could hear the city—distant traffic, the hum of industry, the particular sound of a place that never slept.

"What happens now?" Royce asked.

"Now you tell me who hired you. Who wanted the Harrison family's money. And then you walk away, and you never come back."

"And if I don't?"

Maya didn't answer. She didn't need to.

Royce looked at her for a long moment, and she saw something in his face—recognition, maybe, or respect, or the understanding of one predator for another.

"Fine," he said. "I'll tell you. But you should know—my client isn't someone you can handle. He's connected. Powerful. The kind of man who makes problems disappear."

"I'll take my chances."

"Yes." Royce smiled, a strange expression on a tired face. "I think you would."

He told her everything.
