# Vex

## Background

Vex was born in a city whose name she refuses to speak. It was a port city, wealthy and corrupt, the kind of place where you could buy anything if you knew who to ask and had the coin to pay. Her mother was a servant in a merchant's household; her father was one of the merchant's guards, though which guard remained unclear until Vex's infernal features made the answer obvious. Tieflings aren't common anywhere, but they're especially uncommon in port cities where people have money enough to care about lineage and purity.

Her mother kept her as long as she could, which wasn't long. By seven, Vex was living with an aunt who didn't want her, in a house full of cousins who threw things at her and called her demon-child. By nine, she'd learned to throw things back and to say cutting things that made the adults uncomfortable. By eleven, she'd learned that if you make yourself too much trouble, people stop wanting you around, which is its own kind of freedom.

She left at thirteen. Not kicked out — she chose to leave, told them she was leaving, walked out the door while her aunt stammered protests she didn't mean. She survived on the streets for three years: stealing, charming, occasionally selling things she probably shouldn't have been selling. She learned that being pretty and clever and slightly terrifying was a combination that opened doors. She learned that people expected beautiful women to be stupid, and she learned to use that expectation.

By sixteen, she'd found her way into a network of con artists and thieves that operated in the merchant quarter. She was small, quick, and could pass for human if you didn't look too closely at her eyes or the tiny horns that curved back from her temples. The horns were a problem, so she learned to hide them — scarves, hoods, elaborate hairstyles that drew attention away from her hairline. The red hair she couldn't hide, so she made it into a trademark instead. By eighteen, she was known as "Red Vex," and the name opened as many doors as her face.

The work was satisfying in a way she'd never expected. Not the stealing — the puzzle of it. The study of marks, the construction of elaborate scenarios, the moment when the con came together and the mark handed over what they thought they were getting while she walked away with what they actually had. She was good at it. Better than good. The best in her network, maybe in the city.

She was twenty-three when she got careless. Not with a mark — never with marks — but with information. She'd heard things, you see. Con artists hear things. People talk too much when they think they're being charming, and Vex had a way of being charming that made people forget she was listening. She heard about a shipment, about a noble family, about something being moved through the city that wasn't supposed to be moved at all.

She should have ignored it. She knew she should ignore it. But the information was interesting, and interesting had always been her weakness. She followed the trail. She found the truth. The truth was worse than she'd expected and more valuable than she could have imagined.

The people who owned that truth didn't appreciate her finding it. They sent people. She ran. She ran for six months, from city to city, using every alias and every skill she'd developed. But they were patient, and they were rich, and eventually they stopped chasing and started offering instead. A king's ransom for a woman who knew too much. Delivery to Irongate, where the prisons are deep and the executions are public and no one ever hears from anyone again.

## Personality

Vex is intelligent in a way that has always gotten her into trouble. Not because she's unwise — she's survived this long, which suggests considerable wisdom — but because she can't help herself. She sees patterns, connections, weaknesses. She looks at a person and within minutes knows what they want, what they fear, what lie they're telling themselves. This ability is useful in her work, but it's isolating in her personal life. It's hard to be close to people when you can see exactly what they're thinking and know that if you told them, they'd never speak to you again.

She copes with this isolation through relentless sociability. She talks constantly — observations, questions, provocations, jokes. The talking fills the silence that would otherwise become unbearable. She learned early that if she's talking, she doesn't have to listen to her own thoughts, and her own thoughts are often darker than she wants to examine.

Her humor is sharp enough to cut. She uses it as a defense mechanism, deflecting genuine connection with wit, keeping people at arm's length with well-placed barbs. She doesn't mean to be cruel — well, sometimes she means to be cruel, when cruelty is deserved — but the sharpness is automatic now, a reflex she's never quite learned to control.

There's a loneliness underneath the performance that she rarely acknowledges. She's never kept a friend for more than a year. She's never had a relationship that wasn't transactional in some way. The closest she came was with a fellow con artist named Sariel, who she worked with for two years and might have loved, except that Sariel died in a raid three years ago and Vex doesn't talk about her at all, ever, not even to herself.

Her relationship with her tiefling heritage is complicated. She hates the way people look at her — the fear, the revulsion, the occasional attraction that's itself a kind of objectification. But she's also proud of what she is, in a defiant way. Demons, she thinks, at least knew what they were. Humans pretend to be better and aren't. Her infernal blood is honest in ways that human blood isn't.

## Idiosyncrasies and Flaws

Vex has a habit of touching her horns when she's nervous. The gesture is small — just a finger trailing along the curve — but she's aware of doing it and aware that it makes her look more demonic, which is probably why she does it. There's a defiant pleasure in reminding people what she is.

She can't stand silence. If a conversation lulls, she fills it — with questions, with observations, with deliberate provocations designed to get a reaction. She's aware that this tendency makes her exhausting to be around. She's also aware that she doesn't care, or pretends not to care, or tells herself she doesn't care because caring would be unbearable.

She's vain about her hair. The red is natural, thick, down to her waist when she lets it down. She's spent years learning to style it, braid it, use it as a distraction or a frame or a weapon. She knows it's beautiful, and she uses that beauty the way she uses everything else — as a tool, as a mask, as something that keeps the real her hidden underneath.

Her flaw is that she doesn't believe anyone could genuinely care about her. Every relationship is a transaction in her mind — what she provides, what she receives, when the balance shifts. When Caden starts showing signs of caring, her first instinct is to exploit it. Her second instinct is to убегать (run away). Her third instinct, the one she fights against, is to believe it, and the belief is more terrifying than any exploitation could be.

She also doesn't know when to stop pushing. Every boundary is a challenge, every limit is a gauntlet thrown down. She pushed Sariel too hard, once, in a con that went wrong, and Sariel paid for it with her life. Vex tells herself it wasn't her fault, that Sariel made her own choices, that the raiders were responsible. But the guilt is there, underneath the performance, and it surfaces in dreams she doesn't remember in the morning but that leave her shaken for hours.

## Physicality

Vex is small — five feet and three inches — with a frame that's more wiry than curvy, though people often assume she's delicate because of her height. She's stronger than she looks; years of climbing, running, and fighting have given her muscles that hide beneath pale skin. Her body is a tool she's trained and maintained, and she's appropriately practical about it.

Her face is what draws attention first. Large eyes in a shade of amber that's almost gold, with pupils that are vertically slit in a way that most humans don't have but tieflings often do. The eyes give her an intensity that makes people uncomfortable, especially when she's looking at them like she's taking their measure. Her cheekbones are high and sharp, her mouth is wide, and she has a habit of pressing her lips together when she's thinking, which is often.

The horns are small — maybe two inches, curving back from her temples in a way that could almost pass for unusual hair if you didn't look closely. She usually hides them, but since her capture, she's stopped bothering. Let them see, she thinks. Let them remember what she's capable of.

Her skin is pale with a faint reddish undertone that becomes more noticeable in warm weather or when she's angry. She has freckles across her nose and shoulders, remnants of a childhood spent in a city that was cloudier than this one. Her hands are small, with long fingers good for picking locks and carding through hair and mapping the lines of someone's face.

She moves with a fluid grace that she deliberately cultivates — part performance, part genuine physical intelligence. When she walks, she sways slightly, drawing attention to her hips. When she stands, she tilts her head in a way that exposes her throat, which is either submission or a challenge depending on how you read it. When she sits, she folds herself into small spaces in ways that suggest she's used to being invisible.

Her voice is her other weapon. It's lower than people expect from someone who looks like her, with a contralto range that she can modulate from honeyed to cutting in a single sentence. She's learned to use her voice the way other people use weapons — to disarm, to distract, to get what she wants without anyone realizing they've given it.

She smells like leather and something faintly sweet, maybe sandalwood, maybe just the product she uses in her hair. The leather is from the cuffs she's been wearing for weeks now, and underneath it, there's the smell of her own skin, which she hasn't been able to wash properly since her capture. She's acutely aware of this, and it mortifies her in ways she won't admit.

## Motives, Allegiances, Relationships

Vex's primary motive is survival. She didn't survive eighteen years on the streets and another seven as a con artist by being stupid about danger. She wants to live. She wants to keep living. The desire is basic and animal and underneath all her wit and performance, it's the only motive that matters.

She has no allegiances. Her network is dead or scattered. Sariel is gone. The people she used to work for have written her off as a loss. She has no kingdom, no cause, no loyalty to anything except her own continued existence. This isolation was easier before Caden, when she could tell herself it was a choice rather than a sentence.

Her relationships are all in the past tense. There's no one looking for her, no one who'll pay ransom, no friend who'll come riding to the rescue. She's a woman with no connections being transported to a prison that specializes in making people disappear. She's never felt more alone than she does in this cart, and the alone-ness is its own kind of terror.

But Caden is something. She doesn't know what yet. He's the first person in months who's looked at her like she's a person rather than a problem. He's the first person in years who's made her laugh — genuinely laughed, not the performance laugh she deploys to put people at ease. He's the first person in so long she's lost count who seems to see her without wanting to own her or destroy her or use her for something.

She doesn't trust it. She doesn't trust him. But she's starting to want it anyway, and the wanting is terrifying in ways that capture and execution aren't.

## Voice Experiments

*"You're not going to ask me what I'm in for? Everyone asks. It's practically the first thing out of their mouths. 'What did you do?' Like I owe them an explanation. Like they're the jury already."*

*(He doesn't answer, just watches her with those grey-green eyes, and she finds herself filling the silence anyway, which is exactly what she hates doing, which is exactly what he probably knows.)*

*"I had a friend once. We worked together for two years. She was better at some things than I was, worse at others. We were going to retire. Find a nice coastal town, live out our days in comfort. She died instead. Bad luck. Bad timing. My fault, probably, though I never figured out exactly how."*

*(She didn't mean to say that. The words just came out, and now he's looking at her with something that might be concern, and she hates it, she hates it, she—)*

*"Do you have anyone? Waiting for you, I mean. Back in Thornwall. A wife, maybe. Children. Someone who remembers your name and looks forward to your return."*

*(She doesn't know why she's asking. She doesn't want to know the answer. She's asking anyway, because she's never been able to stop herself from reaching toward the things she shouldn't want.)*

*"You're going to deliver me to Irongate. I know that. You know that. It's your job, and you're very good at your job, and I respect that. But you're also looking at me like you want to eat me alive, and I want to know if you're going to do anything about it or if I'm going to spend the next three days dying of curiosity."*

*(That was too much. She knows it was too much. But his face does something she can't read, and the silence stretches, and she realizes she might have miscalculated, or she might have won — she can never tell the difference until it's too late.)*

# Chapter 1: The Cart

The cart had a squeak in its left wheel that Caden had been ignoring for three hours. He knew he should stop and look at it — the axle might be wearing, the nut might be working loose — but stopping meant giving her more of his attention than the job required, and he wasn't willing to do that. Not yet. Not while he still had walls.

Behind him, in the cage bolted to the cart's bed, the tiefling was talking.

"I could tell you were the quiet type," she said. Her voice carried the particular texture of someone who'd spent years learning to use it — contralto, honeyed, with an edge underneath that suggested the honey was a trap. "Not from the bounty notice. Wenna's notices never say anything useful. Just from the way you looked at me when you put me in here. The efficiency. The careful distance. You were already building your walls."

Caden didn't answer. He'd learned in twelve years that prisoners talked more when you didn't encourage them. Most of them ran out of steam eventually.

She didn't run out of steam.

"My name's Vex, by the way. I suppose Wenna didn't tell you that either. She never does. It's part of the service, I think — keeping the cargo anonymous so you don't get curious." A pause. The cart rocked slightly; she'd shifted her weight against the bars. "But you're curious anyway. I can tell. You checked my chains twice yesterday. That's not procedure. That's you wondering if I could get out if you weren't paying attention."

"You couldn't," he said. The words came out shorter than he'd intended, and he felt the small satisfaction of having spoken at all drown in the knowledge that she'd drawn him out on the first day.

"Probably not," she agreed. "But you didn't know that. You had to check. That's interesting."

The road wound through a stretch of burnt forest — old fire, years past, the blackened stumps spr薄 new growth that looked almost obscene against the ash. Caden watched the horizon while she talked, scanning the tree line for movement. The pay for this job was unusual: three times the standard rate, with a bonus for prompt delivery. Unusual rates meant unusual risks, and he'd learned to respect unusual rates.

"Why a tiefling?" she asked. "Not that I mind being carted around by a competent man — I appreciate competence, truly — but you seem like the type who takes any job that pays, and most of your colleagues would have hesitated at the species. Tieflings make people nervous."

"You make people nervous," he corrected. "Species has nothing to do with it."

"Lie." But she said it like a compliment, with a warmth that made his shoulders tighten. "You were nervous when you opened the cage. I watched you. The way you held yourself, the way you checked the chains before you locked them. You weren't afraid of what I might do. You were afraid of what you might feel. That's different."

Caden turned Sable slightly, bringing the horse to the edge of the road where the going was rougher but he didn't have to look at her. The chestnut mare tolerated the shift with the patience of nine years of partnership, her ears flicking back to track the cart behind them.

The tiefling — Vex — had red hair. That was the first thing he'd noticed when Wenna opened the cage for inspection, before the chains and the contract and the three-day timeline. Hair the color of autumn leaves, of autumn fire, of blood on snow. It fell past her shoulders in waves that looked like they'd been brushed a hundred times, and they probably had; prisoners with nothing to do often preened. Her skin was pale with a faint reddish undertone, like milk left too long in the sun, and her eyes were the gold of a particular kind of trouble he'd learned to recognize.

The horns were small — two inches, curving back from her temples — but they were there, and they were red, and they made the cattle on nearby farms low nervously when the cart passed. Tiefling. Infernal blood. The devil's mark visible on the outside for anyone who knew what to look for.

"You haven't asked what I did," she said. The words came quieter now, less performance, more something he couldn't name. "Everyone asks. It's practically the first question out of their mouths. 'What did you do?' Like I owe them an explanation. Like they're the jury already."

"I don't need to know."

"Don't you?"

He didn't answer. The truth was that he didn't need to know — the contract was clear, the destination was fixed, the reasons were none of his business. He'd carried prisoners who were innocent and prisoners who were guilty and prisoners whose guilt or innocence was so tangled with politics and power that the distinction didn't matter. He'd learned not to ask because asking meant caring, and caring meant the walls got cracks in them.

But she was looking at him now. He could feel it, even with his back turned. The weight of her attention was a physical thing, like standing too close to a fire.

"I stole something," she said. "Does that surprise you? A con artist stealing. How original. But it wasn't money. It wasn't jewels. It was information. I heard things I wasn't supposed to hear, and I didn't know when to stop listening, and now I'm going to Irongate because some very powerful people decided I knew too much to let live."

Caden's hands tightened on the reins. Irongate. He'd heard the name before, of course — everyone in this part of the kingdom had. The fortress-city carved into the mountain, its prisons carved deeper still. They didn't just execute there; they made executions into spectacles, public lessons about what happened to people who crossed the crown. The fact that her destination was Irongate told him everything he needed to know about the severity of her crime.

"King's ransom," he said. It wasn't a question.

"King's ransom," she confirmed. "That was the original offer. Then it got tripled. Then doubled again. Someone very much wants me delivered alive." A pause. "Though I suppose you know that. The contract was specific about that part. No damage to the cargo. No early deaths. Deliver me breathing, or don't deliver me at all."

He had the contract in his saddlebag. Seven pages of careful language specifying her condition on delivery, the payment terms, the penalties for failure. The penalties were severe enough that he hadn't questioned them. He was beginning to wish he had.

"You could let me go," she said. The words were light, conversational, like she was commenting on the weather. "Not here. Not now. But somewhere along the road. The woods are thick, and you're one man, and I'm told you're not young enough to chase me very far. You could take the horse and ride, collect the bounty later, pretend you lost me to bandits. No one would know."

Caden turned in the saddle. He hadn't meant to look at her, but the words pulled his head around like a rope tied to his chin.

She was sitting in the cage with her knees drawn up, her chains looped around her ankles and wrists in patterns that suggested she knew exactly how to escape if she had the chance. Her hair caught the afternoon light and burned like something dying. Her eyes — those gold, slit-pupil eyes — met his with an intensity that made his chest tight.

"I'm not going to let you go," he said.

"No," she agreed. "I didn't think so. You're too careful for that." A smile, small and sharp and something else underneath — respect, maybe, or recognition. "But I had to ask. Had to know if you were the kind of man who could be bought or the kind who couldn't. Now I know."

The road stretched ahead, dust rising in lazy clouds from Sable's hooves. Two more days to Irongate. Maybe three, if the weather held. Caden turned back to the horizon and tried not to think about what she knew now, what she'd seen in his face when he looked at her, what she was already doing to the walls he'd spent twenty years building.

The cart squeaked. The tiefling was quiet. The sun beat down on a man who was beginning to understand that he'd made a mistake somewhere, though he couldn't say exactly where.
step_outputs.7:
# Chapter 2: The Holes in His Armor

The narrow pass was a slash in the hillside, old stone walls crumbling at the edges where roots had pushed through. Caden had ridden this road before, years ago, but the memory was vague — a suggestion of stone rather than the reality of it, walls rising on either side so close that Sable's harness scraped against the rock.

"Stop," Vex said.

He didn't stop.

"I mean it. The walls are too close. If I could reach through the cage, I could touch both sides. That's not safe for the horse."

Caden slowed Sable anyway, not because she'd asked but because she was right. The pass was narrower than he remembered, or maybe the walls had crumbled further in the intervening years. He could feel the stone scraping inches from his knee, could smell the damp of old mortar and newer earth.

"There," he said. "Better?"

"Much." Her voice came from behind him, closer than expected; she'd pressed her face to the bars to be heard. "You actually listened. That's unusual. Most men would have ignored me just to prove a point."

"I'm not most men."

"No," she agreed. "I noticed."

The pass opened onto a river crossing — not a bridge, just a shallow ford where the water ran grey and cold over smooth stones. Caden dismounted to lead Sable through, one hand on the reins, the other on his sword. The water came to his knees, cold enough to make his muscles tense, and he felt Vex's eyes on him from the cart as it rocked through the current behind them.

"Your shoulder," she said when he'd mounted again. "You favor it."

He didn't answer.

"It's the left one. You keep it stiff, even when you're not in the saddle. Old injury?"

"The river's cold," he said. "You should have mentioned if you were cold. I have a blanket."

"I wasn't talking about the river."

The road climbed after the crossing, switching back in the old way that mountain roads did, gaining elevation in increments that Sable's lungs appreciated. The air grew thinner, colder, carrying the scent of pine and something else — rain, maybe, or the promise of it. Caden watched the sky darken to the west and wondered if they'd find shelter before the storm hit.

"You don't talk about yourself," Vex said. She'd shifted in the cage, her chains clinking, her red hair bundled in her hands as she rebraided it from the damp of the river crossing. "That's a choice. Most people talk about themselves constantly — their jobs, their families, their hopes and fears. You don't. Why?"

"I'm not most people."

"You said that already." A smile in her voice, quick and sharp. "I'm beginning to think it's the only thing you know how to say."

Caden turned in the saddle, just for a moment, and caught her looking at him with an expression he couldn't read. Interest, maybe. Assessment. The same look she'd given him when he'd first opened the cage, like she was taking his measure and finding it wanting.

"I have a scar," he said. He didn't know why he was saying it. The words just came. "On my arm. From a prisoner, seven years ago. A woman with a hidden blade. She opened me from elbow to wrist."

Vex's hands stilled in her hair. "Did you kill her?"

"Yes."

"Good." The word was flat, final, without the warmth he might have expected. "Good. She would have killed you otherwise. You know that, don't you? She wouldn't have stopped at a scratch."

"I know."

"So you killed her, and you kept working, and you kept carrying prisoners, and you never let yourself think about whether any of them might be her. Different woman, same blade, same desperation." She met his eyes. "That's not strength. That's cowardice. You're afraid to feel anything, so you don't."

The words landed harder than they should have. Caden turned back to the road, jaw tight, and said nothing.

The rain started an hour before sunset. Not the gentle kind that softened the road and cooled the air, but the hard kind that turned dust to mud and visibility to nothing. Caden pulled his collar up and pushed Sable forward, trusting the horse's instincts more than his own eyes, and behind him Vex had retreated into the cage's far corner where the boards gave some shelter from the worst of it.

The ruin appeared out of the grey like a ghost — a watchtower, old, half-collapsed, its upper stories open to the sky. Caden had passed it before without noticing, but tonight it was salvation. He guided the cart into what had once been a courtyard, unhitched Sable and tied her to a remaining post with a handful of dried grass, and turned to deal with his cargo.

Vex was standing in the cage, gripping the bars, her hair plastered to her face and her clothes dark with rain. "Took you long enough," she said.

"Shut up."

He opened the cage door and she stepped out, her chains clanking, her movements stiff from hours in the confined space. He kept ahold of the chain that bound her wrists — always keep ahold, that was the first rule — but he let her walk on her own into the tower's relative dryness.

The interior was better than the exterior suggested. The walls came up to chest height everywhere, and in one corner a partial roof remained, dry enough to sit on. Caden found a dry spot and gestured for her to sit. She did, folding herself into a compact shape that made her look smaller than she was, her chains arranged around her like a nest of iron serpents.

"I'll need to check the chains," he said. "Make sure the rain didn't rust them."

"Checking the chains." Her voice was flat. "Is that what we're calling it now?"

Caden knelt in front of her and examined the iron, running his fingers along the metal, checking the manacles at her wrists and ankles. The touch was professional, efficient, necessary — until his fingers brushed the skin of her inner wrist and he felt her flinch.

"Cold," she said. "Your hands. They're like ice."

"I'm cold. We're all cold."

"I'm not talking about the weather."

He looked up at her then, and she was closer than he'd expected, her face half-lit by the grey light from the ruined doorway, her gold eyes catching what little illumination remained. Something passed between them — not quite tension, not quite recognition, but something that made his chest tight and his hands still against her skin.

He finished checking the chains and sat back on his heels. The rain hammered the stones around them, steady and relentless, and somewhere in the tower a drip echoed like a slow heartbeat.

"Why do you do it?" she asked. The question was gentler than before, less probe and more genuine curiosity. "The job, I mean. Carrying prisoners. It's not a noble profession. You could do other things."

"It's honest work."

"Honest." She tasted the word like it was something sour. "You believe that? That what you do is honest?"

"I get paid. I deliver. The transaction is clear."

"The transaction is clear." She shook her head, and her hair, still damp, swung around her face. "You know what I think? I think you do this because it's simple. Because the rules are clear, and the boundaries are set, and you never have to wonder whether you're doing the right thing because someone else decided what the right thing was. You just follow orders. Execute contracts. Deliver cargo." She leaned forward, her chains clinking. "You're not a bounty hunter. You're a coward who's afraid to make his own choices."

The words hit like stones. Caden felt his jaw tighten, felt the old familiar anger rising that he hadn't felt in years, and had to breathe through it before he could speak.

"You don't know anything about me."

"I know you couldn't look at me when you said that." Her voice was quieter now, almost gentle. "I know you checked the chains twice yesterday when you thought I wasn't watching. I know you talked to your horse like you were afraid to talk to anyone who could talk back. I know—" She stopped, pressed her lips together, looked away.

"Know what?"

"Nothing. Forget it."

The rain continued. The drip continued. Caden sat against a fallen stone and watched the grey light fade to darker grey, and neither of them spoke for a long time.

"Why aren't you afraid?" he asked finally. The question had been building since the river, since the pass, since the first moment she'd opened her mouth and started filling the silence with words. "You're going to Irongate. You know what happens there. Most prisoners scream the whole way. Cry. Beg. You haven't done any of that."

Vex was quiet for a moment. Then she shifted, pulling her knees up to her chest, wrapping her arms around them like she was holding herself together.

"I was afraid," she said. "For months. After they caught me. While I was waiting in the cell in Thornwall, not knowing if they'd kill me or sell me or what. I was afraid then. But fear runs out eventually. You use it all up, and what's left is just... this." She gestured at herself, at the chains, at the ruined tower. "Acceptance, maybe. Or exhaustion. I don't know the difference anymore."

"That doesn't answer the question."

"Doesn't it?" She looked at him, and for the first time, she didn't look sharp or assessing or performative. She just looked tired. "You asked why I'm not afraid. I'm not afraid because I've already lost everything. There's nothing left to lose, so what's the point of being afraid? But you—" She stopped, shook her head. "You still have something to lose. I can tell. You have something you're afraid of losing, and that's why you're afraid of me."

Caden didn't answer. He didn't have an answer.

The rain eased, then stopped. The clouds broke, showing a sky deepening toward purple and black, and somewhere distant a bird called once and was silent.

"Tell me something true," he said. The words surprised him as much as they seemed to surprise her. "Not a test. Not a manipulation. Just... something true."

Vex was quiet for a long moment. Then she exhaled, slowly, and something in her posture shifted.

"I had a friend," she said. "Her name was Sariel. We worked together for two years. She was..." A pause, a shaky breath. "She was better at some things than I was. Worse at others. We were going to retire. Find a nice coastal town, live out our days in comfort. She died instead. Bad luck. Bad timing." Another pause. "My fault, probably, though I never figured out exactly how."

The words hung in the air between them. Caden didn't know what to do with them — whether to accept them, question them, offer the hollow comfort of apology. He did nothing. He just sat with the weight of her truth and felt it settle into his chest like a stone.

"I had a horse once," he said eventually. He didn't know why he was saying it. The words just came. "Good horse. Better than this one, probably. Bandits took him when they took everything else. I was twenty. I cried about it. Haven't cried since. Probably ran it out of tears somewhere along the way."

Vex looked at him then, and her expression was complicated — something that might have been recognition, might have been pity, might have been both.

"Probably," she agreed quietly.

The night settled around them. The stars came out, cold and distant, and somewhere far away a wolf howled at the moon. Caden leaned his head back against the stone and watched the light fade, and beside him the tiefling woman who was worth a king's ransom closed her eyes and slept.

He watched her for a long time after that. Assessing threat, he told himself. Just assessing threat.

But his hands were still, and his chest was tight, and he couldn't have said what he was really watching for.

# Chapter 3: First Cracks

She woke screaming.

It was barely sound — more of a gasp, a choked-off cry that cut through the ruins like a blade — but Caden was already awake, had been awake for hours, watching the stars wheel overhead and trying not to think about the woman sleeping three feet away from him. He sat up slowly, hands out, the professional posture of a man who knew that waking prisoners required care.

Vex was curled tight against the tower wall, her chains tangled around her like a cocoon, her face contorted in something that wasn't quite waking and wasn't quite dreaming. Her lips moved but no words came, just shapes, syllables that might have been names or might have been pleas.

"Vex." His voice was rough with sleep he hadn't真正 taken. "Vex. Wake up."

She woke like an animal — sudden, violent, her body snapping from the dream-state into alertness before her eyes even focused. For a moment she didn't know where she was, who he was, what the chains meant, and in that moment she looked at him with a terror so raw that he felt it like a blow.

Then she knew. The fear drained away, replaced by something that might have been embarrassment, might have been something worse.

"Sorry," she said. Her voice was hoarse. "I didn't mean to—"

"It's fine."

"It happens sometimes. The dreams. I didn't used to have them, but since the cell, since Thornwall—" She stopped, pressed her lips together, looked away. "It doesn't matter."

He didn't say anything. The professional thing would have been to accept her answer and move on, to not probe at wounds that weren't his concern. But the professional thing had been slipping for hours now, and he found himself asking anyway.

"What do you dream about?"

Vex was quiet for a long moment. Then she shifted, pulling herself into a sitting position, her chains clinking as she arranged her limbs. The first grey light of dawn was creeping through the ruined doorway, painting the stones in shades of shadow and pale.

"Sariel," she said. "Usually. The night she died. We were in a safe house — thought it was safe, anyway — and they came at dawn. Bandits, we thought at first. But they weren't bandits. They were professionals. They knew what they were doing." Her voice was flat, affectless, like she was describing something that had happened to someone else. "I got out through the window. She didn't. I heard her scream and I kept running and I didn't look back."

Caden felt the weight of the confession settle into his chest. "You think about it every night?"

"No. Only when I'm unlucky." A smile that didn't reach her eyes. "Or when I'm unlucky enough to sleep near someone who'll actually notice."

He didn't have an answer for that. He pulled himself to his feet, joints stiff from the hard ground, and began the ritual of breaking camp — check the horse, check the equipment, check the chains. His hands were steady but his mind wasn't, and he could feel her watching him as he worked.

"You're good at that," she said. "The not-talking. The distance. You've had a lot of practice."

"A lot of years."

"How many?"

He paused, saddle in hand. "Twelve. Carrying prisoners. Longer than that doing other things."

"Other things." Her voice was knowing. "Other violent things."

"Some."

"Did you ever kill someone who didn't deserve it?"

The question was too sharp, too pointed, and Caden felt it land somewhere in his chest. He tightened the girth, checked the buckle, avoided her eyes.

"Everyone deserves something," he said. "Whether they get it or not is a different question."

"Philosophical. I didn't take you for philosophical."

"I'm full of surprises."

She laughed — a short, surprised sound that seemed to catch her off guard as much as him. Then she was standing, her chains adjusted, her posture almost casual, and he realized she was closer than she'd been before.

"The road's long," she said. "You might as well tell me something else true. Something you haven't told anyone. Something you're carrying around in that careful silence of yours."

He turned to face her. The dawn light put gold in her hair and gold in her eyes and made her look almost human except for the horns, the faint red in her skin, the way her smile was both promise and threat.

"Why do you care?"

"I don't know." The admission seemed to surprise her. "Maybe I'm curious. Maybe I'm bored. Maybe—" She stopped, shook her head. "It doesn't matter. You don't have to answer."

But he was answering anyway, the words coming out before he could stop them.

"I had a father," he said. "Cooper. Good man. Quiet. He never hit me, never raised his voice, never did anything but look at me with this quiet disappointment that I couldn't earn enough to fill. I left when I was eighteen. I've been leaving ever since. Running away from the look, I mean. The disappointment." He paused, shook his head. "I don't know why I told you that."

"Because you needed to tell someone." Her voice was gentler than he'd heard it. "Because you've spent twelve years building walls and you've never let anyone behind them, and I'm stuck in a cage three feet away, and I'm the only person who'll ever listen."

The road to Irongate was longer than expected. Three days, Wenna had said, but the mountains rose steeper and the valleys deeper, and by midday Caden was calculating the mileage and realizing they'd need a fourth day, maybe a fifth.

Vex had been quieter since the morning — not silent, never silent, but her questions had slowed, her provocations had gentled. She asked about his childhood, his work, the places he'd been. He answered in fragments, the professional caution fighting with something else, something that wanted to be known.

"Did you ever love anyone?" she asked in the afternoon heat. "Really love them. Not just want them. Love them."

The question was so simple, so naked, that Caden felt it land like a blow to the chest. He thought of Pavel, the friend who'd died with an arrow in his throat. He thought of his mother, who he'd never known. He thought of the women and men he'd briefly touched and quickly left.

"No," he said. "I don't think I have."

"I think I did." Her voice was distant. "With Sariel. I think what I felt for her was love, though I never told her. Never had the chance. And now—" She stopped. "It doesn't matter. Now is not the time for that conversation."

The attack came an hour before sunset.

They were moving through a stretch of forest — pine and birch mixed, the ground thick with fallen needles — when Caden heard it: hooves on the trail behind them, more than one rider, moving fast. He turned Sable, hand on his sword, and saw them emerge from the tree line.

Three men. Mounted. Leather armor, scarred faces, the look of men who'd made their lives in the spaces between law and consequence. Bounty hunters, or close enough.

"Evening," the leader called. He was older than the others, with a scar that split his face from forehead to jaw. "We hear you're carrying something valuable. A tiefling, red hair. Worth a king's ransom, they say."

"Her contract is spoken for," Caden said. His voice was calm, professional. "Move along."

The leader laughed. "Contract. Spoken for. You know what happens to men who get possessive about cargo?" He drew his sword, and the others followed suit. "We kill you. We take the woman. We collect the bounty. That's how this works."

Caden had been in this situation before. More times than he could count. The pattern was always the same: men who thought numbers meant safety, who didn't understand that one man who knew what he was doing could be more dangerous than three who didn't.

"Stay in the cart," he said to Vex.

"Like hell I will—"

"Stay in the cart."

He dismounted. The lead bounty hunter came forward, confident, careless, and Caden met him with a cut that opened his shoulder and sent him screaming from the saddle. The second came faster, warier, and Caden caught his blade on a parry and twisted into a thrust that found the gap in his armor. The third tried to circle around, aiming for the cart, and Caden intercepted him with a strike that knocked him from his horse and left him gasping in the dirt.

It was over in minutes. Three men, three horses, three bodies on the forest floor. Caden wiped his blade and checked the living — the leader was still breathing, barely, and would need a healer — and that's when he saw Vex.

She was standing beside the cart, her chains still on but her hands free of the cage. She'd unchained his sword for him during the fight. He hadn't seen her do it, hadn't felt her hands on his blade, but here it was in its sheath, and she was looking at him with something in her eyes that made his chest tight.

"You didn't have to do that," he said.

"Yes, I did." Her voice was quiet. "You'd have died otherwise. I couldn't—" She stopped, shook her head. "I don't know what I was thinking."

He took a step toward her. She didn't move away.

"You saved my life."

"You'd have done the same."

"Would I?"

The question hung between them. Vex looked at him, and the gold of her eyes was unreadable, and the silence stretched like a held breath.

"I don't know," she admitted. "I don't know what you'd do. That's—" Another pause. "That's not something I usually say."

The sun was setting. The forest was darkening. Somewhere in the distance, a bird called once and was silent.

"Breaking the contract," Caden said. His voice was rough. "She asked if he'd ever broken his contract. Delivered a prisoner somewhere other than the agreed destination."

"I heard." Her chains clinked as she shifted. "You didn't answer."

"I know."

"Was that because the answer was no, or because the answer was something you didn't want to say?"

Caden looked at her — really looked, not the professional assessment but something else, something that saw her as a person rather than cargo. She was small, fierce, damaged in ways he couldn't name. She was worth a king's ransom. She was worth less than that, maybe, if you measured in coins. She was worth more than anything, he was beginning to realize, and the realizing terrified him.

"I don't know," he said. "I don't know what I'd do. I don't know what I'm becoming. I don't—" He stopped, shook his head. "I don't know anything anymore."

Vex was quiet for a moment. Then she smiled — small, uncertain, nothing like the sharp smiles she'd worn before.

"That's usually when things get interesting," she said.

The night closed around them. They gathered the horses, secured the prisoners, continued down the road toward Irongate. Caden led the way, and Vex followed in the cart, and neither of them spoke for a long time.

But something had shifted. Something had cracked. And in the cracks, something was beginning to grow.

# Chapter 4: First

The inn appeared at the end of a long afternoon of climbing, a stone building tucked into a mountain pass where the road widened into a trading post. The sign hanging from a rusty hinge read THE HALFWAY HOUSE in letters so weathered they were almost illegible. Caden had stayed here once before, years ago, on a different job with a different prisoner. The memory was vague — a bed, a meal, silence — but the building was the same, solid and unremarkable and exactly what they needed.

He pulled the cart around back, away from the stable where a few horses stood cropping hay, and began the ritual of securing the cargo. Vex waited in the cage, watching him work, her red hair tangled from the road and her gold eyes catching the fading light.

"Not a prison wagon," she observed. "That's unusual. Most of your colleagues use the barred carts. You know, the kind where everyone can see the prisoner. Stare at the demon. Feel superior."

"You're not a demon."

"No. I'm something worse. I'm a woman who knows things."

The innkeeper was a thin woman with grey hair pulled back in a severe bun and eyes that looked like they'd seen everything and found most of it wanting. She glanced at Vex with the usual mixture of fear and fascination that tieflings inspired, then looked at Caden with something sharper.

"Room for two," she said. It wasn't a question.

"Two beds," Caden corrected. "And a locked cage for the prisoner. I'll pay extra."

"Always do."

The room was small but clean, with two narrow beds pressed against opposite walls and a window that looked out on the mountain road. Caden secured Vex to the bedpost — a heavy iron ring bolted into the stone — and stepped back to assess his work. She was chained to the wall, close enough to sit on the bed but not to reach the door. Safe. Contained. Professional.

"You're very thorough," Vex said.

"I'm very careful."

"You're terrified."

He didn't answer. He was terrified. He'd been terrified since the attack in the forest, since she'd unchained his sword for him, since the moment when he'd looked at her and seen something that wasn't a prisoner anymore. The walls were cracking, and he didn't know how to stop the damage.

"You should eat," he said. "I'll bring something up."

"I'm not hungry."

"You should eat anyway."

He left before she could argue. The stairs creaked under his weight, and the common room was warm with firelight and the smell of stew, and he found a corner table and sat with his back to the wall and tried not to think about what was happening in the room above.

The stew was good — thick with root vegetables and salted pork, the kind of meal that filled the belly and warmed the bones. He ate slowly, savoring each bite, prolonging the time he didn't have to spend with her. But eventually the bowl was empty and the fire had burned low and the innkeeper was eyeing him with the knowing look of a woman who'd seen a thousand men in his position.

"Problem upstairs?" she asked.

"No."

"Liar." She wasn't unkind about it. "That woman in chains. She's got you tied in knots. I can see it from here."

"She's cargo. I'm delivering her. There's nothing else."

"There's always something else." She gathered his bowl and moved on, leaving him alone with the words and the silence and the growing certainty that he was going to do something stupid.

He found her sitting on the edge of her bed, her chains looped around the bedpost, her posture suggesting she'd been waiting for him. The room was dim — a single candle on the bedside table — and in the soft light she looked almost human. Almost approachable. Almost not worth a king's ransom.

"Changed your mind about dinner?" he asked.

"Changed my mind about a lot of things." Her voice was low, almost purring. "I've been thinking about what you said. About being afraid of what I might make you do."

"That's not something to joke about."

"I'm not joking." She stood, the chains clinking, and took a step toward him. He didn't step back. "You're afraid of me. I can see it. You're afraid of what I make you feel. You're afraid of wanting something you can't have."

"I don't want—"

"Liar." The word was soft, almost tender. "You've been looking at me for three days. Not like cargo. Not like a demon. Like something else. Something you don't have a word for."

Caden felt his throat tighten. The walls were crumbling. He could feel them falling, one brick at a time, and he didn't have the strength to stop them.

"I'm your prisoner," she said. "You're supposed to deliver me to Irongate. That's the job. That's the contract. Everything else is—" She paused, tilted her head, and her smile was sharp enough to cut. "Everything else is just us."

"You're manipulating me."

"Maybe. Does it matter?"

It shouldn't have mattered. He should have stepped back, should have maintained the distance, should have remembered that she was cargo and he was a professional and this was a job. But his feet wouldn't move and his hands wouldn't obey and she was so close he could smell her — leather and sweat and something sweet underneath, something that made his head swim.

"What do you want?" he asked. The words came out rough. "Really want. Not the job. Not the money. What do you actually want?"

Vex was quiet for a moment. Then she reached up — slowly, giving him time to step back — and touched his face. Her fingers were cool against his cheek, and he felt the touch like a brand.

"I want to feel something," she said. "Before Irongate. Before the end. I want to feel something that's not fear or guilt or this endless waiting. I want—" She stopped, shook her head. "I want you to touch me. I want you to stop pretending you don't want to. I want one night that's just us, not prisoner and captor, not cargo and contract. Just two people who found each other on a road to nowhere."

Caden closed his eyes. Her hand was still on his face, and her thumb was tracing the line of his jaw, and he couldn't remember the last time someone had touched him like this. Not professionally. Not violently. Just... touched.

"I can't," he said.

"Why not?"

"Because I'm supposed to—" He opened his eyes, looked at her, saw the gold of her eyes and the red of her hair and the small curve of her horns and the fear underneath the desire. "Because I don't trust myself with you. Because if I start, I don't know if I can stop."

"Maybe I don't want you to stop."

"You should. You should want me to stop. You should—"

She kissed him.

It wasn't gentle. It wasn't tentative. It was a collision, a crash, two bodies meeting with the force of everything they'd been holding back for three days. Her hands were in his hair and his hands were on her waist and the chains clinked between them as she pulled him closer and he felt the walls finally, finally come down.

"This is a bad idea," he said against her mouth.

"Many of my ideas are bad."

"I should stop."

"You should."

Neither of them stopped.

He didn't know how they made it to the bed. The room was spinning and the candle was guttering and her hands were everywhere — his shoulders, his back, the bare skin where his shirt had come undone. She was laughing and he was groaning and the chains were biting into her wrists but she didn't seem to care and neither did he.

"You have too many clothes," she said.

"You're still chained."

"A problem you could solve."

He shouldn't have. He knew he shouldn't have. But her eyes were gold and her smile was fire and he was reaching for the key before he could think twice. The chains fell away and she was free, and the freedom was its own kind of danger.

"Last chance," he said. "I can still walk away."

"No, you can't."

She was right. He couldn't.

Later, when the candle had burned to nothing and the room was dark except for the moonlight through the window, they lay tangled together in the narrow bed. Vex's head was on his chest and his fingers were tracing the curve of her horn, and neither of them spoke because words would have broken the spell.

"I didn't mean for that to happen," he said finally.

"Liar."

"—"

"You've been wanting it since the river. Since the pass. Since you first looked at me and tried to pretend I was just cargo." She propped herself up on one elbow, looking at him in the dim light. "The question isn't whether it should have happened. The question is what happens now."

Caden thought about Irongate. He thought about the contract in his saddlebag. He thought about the payment waiting on delivery and the reputation he'd built over twelve years of careful distance.

"I don't know," he said. "I don't know what happens now."

Vex smiled — small, uncertain, nothing like the sharp smiles she'd worn before.

"That's usually when things get interesting," she said.

Outside, the moon rose over the mountains. Somewhere in the distance, a wolf howled at the stars. And in a small room in a nameless inn, a man who had spent twenty years building walls finally understood that some walls were meant to be torn down.

He held her tighter, and didn't let go.
step_outputs.10:
# Chapter 5: After

Morning came through the window like an accusation.

Caden woke first, which was a mistake. Waking meant remembering, and remembering meant facing what he'd done. The room was grey with early light, the bed was too narrow for two, and Vex was asleep beside him with her hair spread across the pillow and her bare shoulder rising and falling with each breath.

He lay still for a long moment, watching her. Her face in sleep was softer than it was awake — the sharp angles relaxed, the defensive posture dissolved. She looked young, almost vulnerable, and the horns at her temples looked less demonic and more like the ordinary physical quirk they were. Just a woman. Just a body. Just someone who'd let him—

He stopped the thought before it could finish. Getting up meant facing the day, and the day meant the road, and the road meant Irongate. He could keep her here forever, he knew. The innkeeper wouldn't ask questions; people who paid well didn't get questioned. He could stay in this room, in this bed, and pretend that the contract in his saddlebag didn't exist and the rider looking for her wouldn't come.

But that wasn't how the world worked. That wasn't how any of this worked.

He slipped out of bed as quietly as he could and began gathering his clothes. His shirt was wrinkled from the night before; his trousers bore marks from where he'd kicked them off in haste. He dressed slowly, deliberately, giving himself time to think. The thinking wasn't helping.

"You're thinking too loud."

He turned. Vex was awake, propped on one elbow, the blanket pulled up to her chin. Her gold eyes were only half-open, but there was nothing sleepy in her expression.

"I wasn't trying to wake you."

"You didn't. The guilt woke me." She shifted, sitting up, the blanket falling to reveal the pale skin of her shoulders, the small breasts he'd kissed and tasted and— He looked away. "There's no point in looking guilty. What happened happened. We both wanted it."

"We did."

"Still want it."

He didn't answer. He couldn't answer. The desire was there, pressing against the inside of his ribs, but so was the weight of everything else. The contract. The payment. The job.

"You're going to turn me over." Her voice was flat. Not accusatory. Just factual. "Aren't you?"

Caden turned to face her. She was sitting with her knees drawn up, arms wrapped around them, making herself small. The posture was so different from the woman who'd kissed him last night that he felt the contrast like a physical blow.

"I don't know."

"Bullshit."

"I don't know." The words came out rougher than he intended. "I don't know what I'm going to do. I don't know who I am anymore. I spent twelve years building a life that worked, and you—" He stopped, shook his head. "You broke it. You broke me. And now I don't know what I'm supposed to do with the pieces."

Vex was quiet for a long moment. Then she unfolded herself and stood, reaching for the clothes she'd folded over the chair. She dressed slowly, deliberately, giving him time to watch. He didn't look away.

"You could let me go," she said. "Not here. Not now. But somewhere. The woods are thick, and you're one man, and no one would know."

"I told you before—"

"I know what you told me before. Before was before. This is now." She met his eyes, and there was something in her face he hadn't seen before. Not the sharp performance of the prisoner. Not the desire of the night. Something rawer. More afraid. "I don't want to die, Caden. I didn't before, but I didn't care much either. Now I—" She stopped, pressed her lips together. "Now I have a reason to care."

The words hung between them. Caden felt them land somewhere in his chest and settle there, heavy and permanent.

"A rider," he said finally. "Yesterday. Before we reached the inn. Someone from the faction that paid for your transport. Checking on progress."

Vex's expression shifted. The vulnerability was gone, replaced by something sharper. "What did they say?"

"That we'd be on schedule. That there were no problems. That you'd be delivered within the week."

"And they believed you?"

"They believed the money. That's all that matters to them."

She was quiet for a moment, processing. Then she nodded, once, sharp and decisive. "They're not checking on you. They're checking on me. Making sure I haven't escaped, haven't been killed, haven't found a way to disappear. They don't trust you."

"People like them don't trust anyone."

"No. They don't." She looked at him, and her expression was complicated — respect and calculation and something that might have been affection. "You're playing a dangerous game. Keeping me here. Keeping me... this."

"I didn't plan any of this."

"I know." She stepped closer, close enough that he could smell her — the same leather and sweetness from last night, overlaid with the musk of sleep. "That's what makes it interesting."

They broke camp in silence. Caden secured her to the cart with the chains that suddenly felt obscene after the night before. She didn't comment on the irony. The innkeeper watched them from the doorway, eyes sharp, but said nothing. People who paid well didn't get questioned.

The road wound down from the mountains, dropping into a valley where the trees were thicker and the light was greener. Caden rode in silence, and Vex sat in the cart with her knees drawn up, and neither of them spoke for hours.

The rider came at midday.

He emerged from a side trail on a lathered horse, a man in the colors of a house Caden didn't recognize — dark blue and silver, a crest he couldn't name. The rider pulled up beside the cart and looked Vex over with eyes that made Caden's jaw tighten.

"Progress report," the rider said.

"On schedule." Caden's voice was flat. "Three days to Irongate."

"Any complications?"

"No."

The rider studied him for a long moment. Then he turned his horse and rode on without another word, disappearing down the road as quickly as he'd appeared.

Vex exhaled. "He wasn't checking on progress."

"No."

"He was making sure you hadn't let me go."

"Yes."

"He doesn't trust you."

"No."

She was quiet for a moment. Then: "Why did you tell him the truth?"

Caden turned in the saddle. "What?"

"Why did you tell him we were on schedule? You could have lied. Said we were delayed. Said there were problems. Bought us time." Her gold eyes were steady on his. "Why didn't you?"

"Because lying would have made him suspicious. And suspicious men come back with more men." He paused, looked ahead at the road. "I need time to think. I can't do that if they're watching."

"So you're thinking."

"I'm trying to."

The afternoon stretched long and golden. The road dropped further into the valley, the air warming as the elevation fell. Caden found himself glancing back at the cart more often than he should have, watching Vex's profile against the sky, remembering the way she'd looked in the candlelight.

She caught him looking and smiled — small, private, nothing like the sharp smiles she'd worn before.

"You're thinking again," she said.

"Always."

"About what?"

"You. What happens. What I do." He paused. "What you said this morning. About not wanting to die."

Vex's smile faded. "I meant it."

"I know."

"You should know. I've been trying to die quietly for months. Not actively, not deliberately, just... passively. Letting things happen. Not fighting. Because what's the point, right? No family, no friends, no future. Just a road to Irongate and a cell at the end of it." She was quiet for a moment. "But now there's you. And you're looking at me like—" She stopped, shook her head. "You're looking at me like I'm a person. Like I matter. And I didn't know how much I needed that until you started doing it."

Caden felt the weight of her words settle into his chest. He'd spent his life maintaining distance, keeping people at arm's length, building walls that kept everyone out. He hadn't known what it cost him until the walls came down and someone was finally on the other side.

"You matter," he said. The words came out rough. "Whatever happens. You matter."

"I know." Her voice was soft. "That's the problem."

They made camp that evening in a clearing beside a small stream, the water running clear over stones that caught the last light of the sun. Caden built a fire while Vex sat with her chains wrapped around a tree, watching him work.

"You're good at that," she said. "The domestic stuff. Building fires. Setting up camp. Taking care of things."

"It's just survival."

"It's more than that." She was quiet for a moment. "You take care of your horse. You take care of your equipment. You took care of me last night." Her voice dropped. "It's been a long time since anyone took care of me."

Caden fed a stick to the fire and watched the flames catch. "Sariel didn't take care of you?"

"Sariel and I took care of each other. That's different. That's two people holding each other up because neither of them could stand alone." She paused. "I haven't stood alone since she died. I've just been falling."

"Until now."

"Until now."

The night settled around them. The fire crackled. The stream whispered over stones. Caden sat on one side of the flames and Vex on the other, and the distance between them felt vast and fragile.

"Come here," he said.

She looked at him, a question in her eyes.

"Please."

She moved around the fire, her chains clinking, and sat beside him on the log he'd found. Close enough that he could feel her warmth, far enough that the chains didn't tangle.

"I don't know what I'm going to do," he said. "About Irongate. About the contract. About any of it. But I know that I can't—" He stopped, shook his head. "I can't pretend last night didn't happen. I can't go back to who I was before. You're in my blood now. And I don't know how to get you out."

"Good." Her voice was barely above a whisper. "Don't get me out. Stay messy. Stay confused. Stay—" She reached out, touched his face, the same gesture she'd made in the inn. "Stay with me."

He kissed her then. Slower than last night. Deeper. Less desperate and more deliberate, like they had all the time in the world. They didn't, but for a moment, in the firelight by the stream, it didn't matter.

Later, when the fire had burned low and the stars were out in full force, they lay together in the bedrolls Caden had spread beside the flames. Vex was curled against him, her head on his chest, her fingers tracing idle patterns on his skin.

"I can feel your heartbeat," she said.

"I know."

"It's fast."

"I know."

She was quiet for a moment. Then: "Whatever you decide. About Irongate. About me. I want you to know that it was worth it. This. Us. Whatever we have in whatever time we have." She propped herself up, looked at him in the starlight. "I've spent my whole life running and hiding and pretending I didn't need anyone. And then there was you. And you saw me. The real me. The scared me. The me that's afraid of dying alone in a cell." Her voice broke. "No one's ever seen that before."

Caden touched her face, tracing the line of her jaw, the curve of her horn, the softness of her cheek.

"I'll see you," he said. "I'll keep seeing you. Whatever happens."

She kissed him then, and the kiss was tender and sad and full of everything they couldn't say. And in the morning, when the sun rose over the mountains, they would have to face the road ahead.

But that was later. For now, there was only the fire and the stars and the two of them, tangled together in the dark.
